summary: Nassau County is where my family's old plantation is located, and about six years ago, the county road department put a dirt road right through the center of it. This road runs for three miles and has made access much easier than it used to be. The Zetterow family, who are fourth generation residents here, also have land in Nassau County. My family has been paying taxes and contributing to the upkeep of Nassau County for over 150 years.

The Zetterow family has been in Nassau County for four generations. They used to own a large tract of land, about four thousand acres, but have sold most of it now. I remember teaching Oscar Zetterow's father and seeing Oscar and his wife Orb run a filling station and small store here. They are hardworking people, running their business day and night, and Oscar even helped me get some ice for a Georgia watermelon once.

Georgia is just a few miles northeast of where my family plantation lies, with St. Marys being the seat of government during Spanish rule. I have connections there through family, as my great-grandfather Henry O'Neill was granted land when Florida and much of Georgia were under the Spanish Empire. Also, my sister's children now live in New York, but my brother lives in California, showing how far our family has spread from Georgia and Florida.

Jacksonville is the city from which we made our trip to the old plantation, a journey that now takes about an hour but used to take two and a half hours by buggy. Mr. Powell, who lives in Jacksonville, is one of the men contracted to turpentine my land. I also had an accident in Jacksonville at my lifelong friend Mrs. Arthur T. Williams' home, where I broke my ankle six months ago. Jacksonville is an important hub for my family's business and personal connections.

Fernandina Beach is near my family's plantation and is home to Mr. Shave, who, along with Mr. Powell from Jacksonville, manages the turpentine contract on my land. The seat of government during Spanish rule was near here, at St. Marys, Georgia, just a few miles northeast. Fernandina is part of the rich coastal area where my family has lived for generations, and it connects to the waterways like the Amelia River and Clark River that border our property.

Henry O'Neill was my great-grandfather who served the Spanish Crown when Florida and much of Georgia were under Spanish rule. For his distinguished service, his widow was granted over a thousand acres of land, a captain's mansion, and a special award called the Star of Montpelier signed by the King of Spain. Sadly, Henry was killed by a Spanish outlaw, but his legacy lives on through the land and family history. His widow later married William Hollingsworth, linking our family to other prominent local families.

During the time of the Spanish Empire, Florida and a large part of Georgia were under Spanish control, which is when my great-grandfather Henry O'Neill served the Crown. Because of his service, my great-grandmother was granted a large tract of land and a special award from the King of Spain. The seat of government for this region was at what is now St. Marys, Georgia. This period shaped much of my family's history and land ownership in the area.

St. Marys, Georgia, was the seat of government during the Spanish dominion over Florida and parts of Georgia. It lies just a few miles northeast of my family's plantation near Fernandina. My great-grandmother remarried William Hollingsworth of St. Marys, linking our family to that community. Also, William Hollingsworth was honored for his role in the Revolutionary War, which ties our family history closely to that place.

William Hollingsworth was my great-grandmother's second husband after Henry O'Neill died. He was from St. Marys, Georgia, and had two daughters with her, Alisa and Mary. I discovered in an old deed that William Hollingsworth was honored in Duval County for winning a battle during the Revolutionary War at Camp New Hope, which was named after our New Hope Plantation. His legacy is an important part of my family's history.

During the Revolutionary War, my ancestor William Hollingsworth led troops to victory at a battle fought at Camp New Hope, which was named after our New Hope Plantation. This battle was significant enough that a memorial was placed in Duval County to honor his achievement. The war is a proud part of my family's history and connects us to the early struggles of this country.

Camp New Hope was a site named after our New Hope Plantation where my ancestor William Hollingsworth fought a battle during the Revolutionary War. This battle was significant enough to be commemorated with a memorial in Duval County. The camp represents a key moment in my family's military history and their contribution to the war effort.

New York City is where my sister Florence's children now live, far from our family plantation in Florida. Back when our vegetable crops were thriving, produce was shipped all the way to New York markets by barge and freighter. This city represents a distant connection for my family, linking our rural roots to a bustling urban center.

The Amelia River borders part of my son's land, stretching about two miles west to Lanceford Creek, or Clark's River as it's now called. This river is part of the natural boundary of our family property and is visible from the old plantation grounds. I remember the oysters clinging to the roots of a live oak tree in the river, a striking sight that connects the land to the water.

Powell and Have are the men contracted to turpentine my land, with Mr. Powell living in Jacksonville and Mr. Shave in Fernandina. They pay ten cents a tree, which brings in about five hundred dollars a year for me. This contract will last six or seven years until the turpentine is exhausted, after which the trees will be either dead or only good for pulpwood. Their work is an important source of income for maintaining the property.

Clark River, formerly known as Lanceford Creek, runs along the western boundary of my son's 290-acre land. It is a tidal river where you can see oysters clinging to the roots of a large live oak tree that has been eroded by the water. The river marks a significant natural feature of our plantation and adds to the beauty and resources of the land.

Lanceford Creek is the old name for what we now call Clark River, which runs along the edge of my son's land. It was once the site where vegetables grown on the plantation were loaded onto barges and shipped to markets like New York. The creek is part of the rich natural environment that supported our family's agricultural business for many years.

The Avenue leading to our old plantation house is flanked by immense old live oak trees, creating a majestic, shady drive. One particular live oak, called the 'oyster tree,' stands almost in the Clark River with oysters clinging to its roots and branches. These live oaks are a beautiful and historic part of the landscape, marking the entrance to the family home and connecting us to the natural history of the area.

Nueva Esperanza is the name of the old plantation house and property where I was born in 1904. The house was built in 1882 from lumber of the original 1832 home brought by my father, Henry O'Neill. Recently, Mr. and Mrs. Toomer came from Jacksonville and asked to occupy Nueva Esperanza for the summer to make repairs, which I gladly allowed. The place holds many memories and is still a cherished family estate despite its dilapidated condition.

The Toomer family came through Jacksonville and asked permission to stay at Nueva Esperanza for the summer to make repairs to the old plantation house. Mr. Toomer is a musician, and we hoped he might help preserve some of the old plantation songs and spirituals I have collected. They have a boarder who is a carpenter helping with repairs in exchange for room and board. The Toomers have brought new life to the place during their stay.

The War between the States, or the Civil War, came after the glamorous days of my childhood at the plantation. Before the war, my siblings and I led carefree lives here, but the conflict changed everything. The plantation's history and the lives of those who lived here, including the hundred slaves owned by my father, were deeply affected by the war's outcome.
